summary: Atwood highlights an article warning against dogmatic adherence to trendy frameworks at the expense of good design. The post emphasizes that blindly following heavyweight frameworks like EJB forces developers to write code with no business value. It advocates for thoughtful consideration of lightweight alternatives like Hibernate, Spring, and PicoContainer when they can accomplish the required job. The piece concludes with Atwood's characteristic humor, joking that users are the real problem with software development.
key_points: Dogmatic conformity to frameworks can undermine good design and implementation practices
Simple problems deserve simple solutions—public fields can be better than boilerplate getters/setters
Heavyweight frameworks often require writing significant code with no business value
Choose frameworks deliberately rather than following bandwagons or rebelling against them
Consider whether a lighter framework can accomplish the required job before adopting complexity
money_quotes: Your code sucks if it dogmatically conforms to a trendy framework at the cost of following good design and implementation practices.
If a field is transparently readable and writable why not simply make the field public?
In general it's a good thing whenever you can throw out, or avoid writing, some code.
Using a heavy framework generally requires that you must write a significant amount of code that has no business value.
Never blindly adopt a heavy framework just because it's the current bandwagon.
Likewise, don't blindly adopt a lightweight framework in defiance. Always give due consideration to your choices.
The real problem with software development is the users. It's unbelievable. They've caused problems with every program I've ever written.
key_insight: Framework choices should be driven by pragmatic assessment of actual needs, not by dogmatic adherence to trends or reflexive rejection of them.
